content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 11, 2003

George W. Snyder
9751 Merced Falls Road
La Grange, CA 95329

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-03-054

Dear Mr. Snyder:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your letter does not seek advice regarding a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTIONS

1. Will your employment as a consultant for a corporation that will be preparing a development project to be presented to the board for which you serve as a member, cause a conflict of interest for you in your governmental capacity?

2. Will you avoid violating the conflict-of-interest laws if you recuse yourself from the decisionmaking process regarding the corporation's project?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Because the corporation is a source of income to you, you will have a conflict of interest disqualifying you from involvement in governmental decisions concerning the corporation's project if the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the corporation.

_______________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or written advice (Government Code § 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. § 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

File No. 1-03-054
Page No. 2

2. By recusing yourself from these decisions, which entails not making,
participating in making, or otherwise using your official position to influence
the decisions, you will avoid violating the conflict of interest laws.

FACTS

You are currently a board member for the Lake Don Pedro Community Services
District ("District") and have held this position since November 1983. The District was
formed in August 1980 to "supply the inhabitants of the District with water for domestic
use, irrigation, sanitation, industrial use, fire protection, and recreation; and for the
collection, treatment, and disposal of sewage, storm water of the District and its
inhabitants."

Currently, you are receiving payment from a corporation for which you are acting
in the capacity of consultant. This corporation is in the early process of putting together a
development project within the service area of the District. The project will consist of
approximately 875 residential and 125 commercial connections requiring that the District
provide water and sewer service. The District may be asked to provide other services as
well such as parks and recreation, fire and ambulance services, and solid waste disposal.

ANALYSIS

The primary purpose for the conflict of interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that "[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, should perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of
this goal, section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating
in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. Determining whether a conflict of
interest exists under section 87100 requires analysis of the following questions as
outlined below.³ Please note that the Act does not include Government Code section
1090 which may also impose restrictions if there is a contractual arrangement between
the corporation and the District to provide services.⁴

Steps One and Two: Are you considered a "public official" and are you
making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?

As a board member of the District, you are a "member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency" and, therefore, are a public official
subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048; regulation
18701(a).) A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or

³ These questions are based on the Act's conflict-of-interest analysis provided at regulation
18700(b).
⁴ You should consult the Attorney General's office for further guidance on Government Code
section 1090.

File No. I-03-054
Page No. 3

commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See regulation 18702.1, copy enclosed.) You
will "make a governmental decision" if you vote on a matter before the District on
providing services to the proposed development project. Additionally, if you engage in
any of the actions described in enclosed regulations 18702.2 and 18702.3 with regard to
such decisions, you will "participate in making" or "influence" that decision.

Your question indicates that you may recuse yourself from any discussions or
votes on the corporation's project. A conflict of interest will only exist if an official is
making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision as described
above. Thus, assuming that the official with the conflict of interest does not make,
participate in making, or influence a governmental decision in which he or she has the
conflict of interest, that official will have complied with the official's obligations under
the Act.

Step Three: What are your economic interests, the possible sources of a conflict of
interest?

The economic interests that might give rise to a conflict of interest are defined in
regulations 18703-18703.5. The specific economic interest⁵ that applies to the situation
described in your request for advice is your source of income. A public official has an
economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates
to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision at issue. (Section 87103(c);
regulation 18703.3). In the event that you own a consulting business, the income of a
public official also includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust
in which the official owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or
greater. (Section 82030(a).) Thus, sources of income to you personally, or to a business
entity by which you provide consulting services, are considered to be sources of income
to you.

Step Four: Is your economic interest, the company employing you as consultant,
directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decisions?

A person,⁶ including a source of income in which a public official has an
economic interest, is directly involved in a governmental decision if that person, either
directly or by an agent, initiates the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal, or
similar request, or is a named party in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the

⁵ In addition to the economic interests separately listed in section 87103, a public official
has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision
foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a 12-month period.
(Regulations 18703.5 and 18705.5.)

⁶ The term "person" is defined in section 82047 as "an individual, proprietorship, firm,
partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company, corporation, limited liability company,
association, committee, and any other organization or group of persons acting in concert."

File No. I-03-054
Page No. 4

decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(1) - (a)(2).)
A person is the subject of a proceeding if the decision before the agency "involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with," that person. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).) If a person
who is an economic interest to a public official is not directly involved in a governmental
decision, the person is deemed to be indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704.1(b).) When
the corporation applies to the District to provide services, it meets the test for being
directly involved in the governmental decision of whether or not to grant the request for
those services.

Steps Five and Six: Will the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the
governmental decision on your economic interest be material?

The corporation is a source of income to you by virtue of the money you receive
in your capacity as consultant for it. Therefore, any reasonably foreseeable financial
effect at all, even one penny, on the corporation when the corporation is directly involved
in a decision before the District is deemed material. (Regulation 18705.3(a).)

Steps Seven and Eight: "Public Generally" and "Legally Required
Participation" Exceptions

Because you have not presented specific facts that suggest issues on the subjects
of the "public generally" or "legally required participation," we have not gone on to
analyze the latter two steps. Step seven is an exception that applies where the reasonably
foreseeable and material financial effect on the official's economic interest is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. This exception generally does not
apply in cases where economic interests are directly involved in a decision. Step eight is
an exception that applies when the official is legally required to participate in the
decision. We have enclosed regulations 18707, 18707.1, and 18708 for your information.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Adrianne Korchmaros
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures
